findings.message: `user_id` is a personal identifier under GDPR Art. 4(1). It is written to application logs with no documented retention period, no deletion path, and no right-to-erasure mechanism (GDPR Arts. 5(1)(e) and 17). The docstring's claim that 'no PII is logged or stored' is factually incorrect.
findings.suggestion: Either omit `user_id` from the log entirely, or ensure the logging pipeline has a documented retention policy, automatic purge after the retention window, and an erasure handler that scrubs log entries on Art. 17 requests. At minimum, add a retention annotation (e.g. a structured `_retention_days` field) so the log-shipper can enforce it.

findings.message: `name_filter` (user-supplied search term) is logged alongside `user_id`, producing a behavioural record — what this identified user searched for — with no retention limit or erasure path. Even if the filter value is not PII in isolation, tying it to a `user_id` makes the combined log record personal data under GDPR Art. 4(1).
findings.suggestion: If search-term logging is required for analytics or abuse detection, treat the resulting records as personal data: apply the same retention/erasure controls as `user_id`, or hash/truncate the filter before logging so it cannot be linked back to an individual.

findings.message: `name_filter` is accepted as a query parameter and persisted in logs (line 51), but is never forwarded to `list_items()` (line 52). Collecting and storing a data element that serves no functional purpose in the request violates the GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) data-minimisation principle.
findings.suggestion: Either pass `name_filter` to `list_items()` so it is actually used, or remove the parameter entirely. Do not log data that has no functional role in the operation.

findings.message: `user_id` is transmitted as a URL query parameter. Query strings are routinely captured in server access logs, reverse-proxy logs, CDN logs, browser history, and HTTP `Referer` headers — creating undocumented secondary personal-data stores with no retention or erasure policy, in violation of GDPR Arts. 5(1)(e) and 32.
findings.suggestion: Move `user_id` out of the query string: read it from a signed session cookie, a JWT in the `Authorization` header, or a request-body field on this POST endpoint. This confines the identifier to channels where retention policies are already enforced.

findings.message: `list_items(session)` is called without `limit` or `offset`, returning the entire item table. Unlike `GET /items`, this endpoint has no pagination, potentially exposing a disproportionately large data set per request and violating the GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) data-minimisation principle.
findings.suggestion: Apply the same `limit`/`offset` parameters (or at least a hard server-side cap equivalent to `MAX_PAGE_SIZE`) used by `get_items` to bound the response to what is strictly necessary.
